content.content:
# c-mo1mq8ry-c8cf4810db6c [live]
> Unlock autonomous hopper-based operation for Nexus
Status: live
Created by: bae3f417-1473-4b6c-a4e9-fcd9c6c8d384
Created: 2026-04-16T15:23:48.334Z

## c-mo1mq93f-a8d85ce47baf [concluded]
> Redesign Astrolabe planner for intent-and-constraints specs with tiered decision model
Status: concluded
Conclusion: Design closed. The planner redesign resolved into: (1) intent-and-constraints specs (not prescriptive implementation) — concluded children c-mo1uw0ah, c-mo1uw15a; (2) binary pick+record / surface model replacing the originally-proposed 4 tiers — concluded child c-mo1mq9qa (tier model scrapped), c-mo1mq9f6 (preference model dropped as not-MVP); (3) a five-criterion razor for which decisions surface — c-mo1yb8aj; (4) three codified policy defaults that account for ~40% of observed overrides — c-mo1yb8nf; (5) empirical baseline of ~3.7% override rate across 38 specs for future comparison — c-mo1yb8zb; (6) ratification-not-approval as the patron-interaction model — c-mo1uw2ml. Handoff-note design (c-mo1mqa1y) dropped after X010 H1 findings reverted the default engine to single-session implement. Three lines dropped explicitly: seam contracts registry, task type attribute, mandatory terminal verify task. Operational follow-up is draft brief writ w-mo1yp84y-7bf521a80d63 (Update Astrolabe sage instructions with value-laden razor and three codified defaults) covering the four sage roles (reader / reading-analyst / analyst / writer). Review and publish that writ to commission the sage-instruction updates. Shipped infrastructure: v1 task manifest (w-mo0ypqs4) and v2 task-loop engine (w-mo0yqdyr) — the task-loop engine remains opt-in per X010 policy reversion.
Created by: bae3f417-1473-4b6c-a4e9-fcd9c6c8d384
Resolved by: 2d4de462-a884-42b2-b706-8030a536c51c
Created: 2026-04-16T15:23:48.748Z
Resolved: 2026-04-16T20:59:10.661Z

### c-mo1mq9f6-ee8e8da74657 [dropped]
> Design the preference model that graduates patron corrections into planning defaults
Status: dropped
Conclusion: Dropped as not-MVP. Override-rate analysis shows the manual learning loop (patron reads records, updates sage instructions) is already operational; automating correction-graduation offers diminishing returns versus simply codifying Sean's already-expressed policy preferences (see sibling click 'Codifiable policy defaults from override analysis'). Revisit only if override rate plateaus above a threshold that manual codification can't drive down. Decision made in session 2d4de462.
Created by: bae3f417-1473-4b6c-a4e9-fcd9c6c8d384
Resolved by: 2d4de462-a884-42b2-b706-8030a536c51c
Created: 2026-04-16T15:23:49.170Z
Resolved: 2026-04-16T20:47:58.375Z

### c-mo1mq9qa-6c047858cc63 [concluded]
> Design tiered decision model that proceeds on best-guess defaults
Status: concluded
Conclusion: Tier model scrapped. Replaced by a binary pick+record / surface split with a razor (see sibling click 'Razor for value-laden planner decisions'). The 4-tier framing presumed Tier 1-3 plumbing distinctions would be useful MVP design; empirical override analysis of 35 overrides across 38 specs showed (a) the pick/surface binary is the only edge that matters at MVP, and (b) ~40% of what looked like Tier 4 value-judgments are actually codifiable policy preferences Sean has already expressed, not per-decision taste. Tiered plumbing can be designed later if concrete decisions reveal the need. Decision made in session 2d4de462.
Created by: bae3f417-1473-4b6c-a4e9-fcd9c6c8d384
Resolved by: 2d4de462-a884-42b2-b706-8030a536c51c
Created: 2026-04-16T15:23:49.570Z
Resolved: 2026-04-16T20:47:54.449Z

### c-mo1mqa1y-23841a48a938 [dropped]
> Design task session handoff notes for the implement-loop engine
Status: dropped
Conclusion: Dropped. The implement-loop engine these handoff notes were designed for is no longer the default — see c-mo1xoggo (policy: revert default to single-session implement). X010 H1 empirical data (c-mo1vzvjp) showed monolithic is 2.6x cheaper and 3.6x faster than decomposed implement-loop on the Oculus-scale spec, with no quality premium. Handoff notes solve a problem only experienced when session-level decomposition is happening; if decomposition returns — either at larger scales per H4, or via the lazy-decomposition branch c-mo1w08cs — handoff-note design should happen in that context (the related click c-mo1w11jq already exists there under the session-boundary experiment). No design work here is salvageable into the lazy-decomp framing, which has different structural assumptions (agent self-stops mid-work rather than orchestrator-driven task boundaries).
Created by: bae3f417-1473-4b6c-a4e9-fcd9c6c8d384
Resolved by: 2d4de462-a884-42b2-b706-8030a536c51c
Created: 2026-04-16T15:23:49.990Z
Resolved: 2026-04-16T20:54:21.983Z

#### c-mo1mqadm-357fc3048465 [dropped]
> Where should handoff notes be stored — task writ, session record, or dedicated book?
Status: dropped
Conclusion: Dropped with parent c-mo1mqa1y. The question is moot: handoff notes are not an MVP feature after the X010 H1 policy reversion to single-session implement (see c-mo1xoggo).
Created by: bae3f417-1473-4b6c-a4e9-fcd9c6c8d384
Resolved by: 2d4de462-a884-42b2-b706-8030a536c51c
Created: 2026-04-16T15:23:50.410Z
Resolved: 2026-04-16T20:54:15.214Z

#### c-mo1mqap7-2ef29039b1b2 [dropped]
> Should all previous handoff notes be queryable, or just the immediately prior one?
Status: dropped
Conclusion: Dropped with parent c-mo1mqa1y. The question is moot: handoff notes are not an MVP feature after the X010 H1 policy reversion to single-session implement (see c-mo1xoggo).
Created by: bae3f417-1473-4b6c-a4e9-fcd9c6c8d384
Resolved by: 2d4de462-a884-42b2-b706-8030a536c51c
Created: 2026-04-16T15:23:50.828Z
Resolved: 2026-04-16T20:54:16.187Z

### c-mo1uw0ah-06b417bbd201 [concluded]
> Should specs prescribe implementation or describe intent and constraints?
Status: concluded
Conclusion: Intent and constraints. The planner's job is decision closure and context distillation, not predicting code. Overly prescriptive specs suppress implementer judgment and propagate planner errors faithfully. Validated against w-mny1zoc9 — its prescriptive rename table missed the spider zombie reaper; an intent brief naming the cross-cutting concern ("grep the monorepo") with an executable verify field would have caught it. Decision taken in design session 860f69d7; shipped via direct sage-instruction update in session ed5d3df5. Original quest body: docs/archive/quests/w-mo0v636y-41c8aeff857f.json.
Created by: e2eab8bf-426b-4dd3-9c10-ddae870e8271
Resolved by: e2eab8bf-426b-4dd3-9c10-ddae870e8271
Created: 2026-04-16T19:12:14.201Z
Resolved: 2026-04-16T19:12:32.160Z

### c-mo1uw15a-4377fc9722b0 [concluded]
> Does the planner have better information than the implementer?
Status: concluded
Conclusion: No. Both read the same codebase. The planner's enumeration of files, consumers, etc. is no more reliable than the implementer's. Presenting planner enumerations as authoritative actively suppresses the implementer's audit instinct. Name the concern and verification method instead — let the implementer discover the actual set. Decision taken in design session 860f69d7. Original quest body: docs/archive/quests/w-mo0v636y-41c8aeff857f.json.
Created by: e2eab8bf-426b-4dd3-9c10-ddae870e8271
Resolved by: e2eab8bf-426b-4dd3-9c10-ddae870e8271
Created: 2026-04-16T19:12:15.310Z
Resolved: 2026-04-16T19:12:35.617Z

### c-mo1uw22y-9129aa0a62e8 [concluded]
> How should planner decisions be tiered and resolved?
Status: concluded
Conclusion: Four tiers: (1) Hardcoded — obviously correct, no variance expected; (2) Configurable preference — set once, not actively learned; (3) Pluggable + measurable — system picks winner via outcome data; (4) Pluggable + value-gated — requires patron signal. Most decisions just proceed: Tiers 1–3 are made, recorded, and moved forward. Only Tier 4 surfaces for ratification, and even then the system has already proceeded with a default. Decision taken in design session 860f69d7. Original quest body: docs/archive/quests/w-mo0v636y-41c8aeff857f.json.
Created by: e2eab8bf-426b-4dd3-9c10-ddae870e8271
Resolved by: e2eab8bf-426b-4dd3-9c10-ddae870e8271
Created: 2026-04-16T19:12:16.522Z
Resolved: 2026-04-16T19:12:40.840Z

### c-mo1uw2ml-33fceb4a9943 [concluded]
> Should the patron approve decisions before they're made, or ratify them after?
Status: concluded
Conclusion: Ratify after. System proceeds on best guess; surfaces Tier 4 decisions asynchronously with tradeoffs pre-articulated and a default already applied. Sean's sharpest framing: 'Requiring input should be akin to an incident in other systems, a real failure.' This reframes patron interaction from blocking approval to post-hoc ratification. ### c-mo1yb8aj-cec786a3eaa8 [concluded]
> Razor for value-laden planner decisions
Status: concluded
Conclusion: Five criteria, derived from mining 35 patron overrides across 38 specs. A planner decision surfaces to the patron if and only if it is: (1) Vocabulary/pattern establishment — new guild terms, categorical distinctions, patterns other code will follow; (2) Human-facing surface — CLI text, error messages, agent personalities, doc phrasing, UX details; (3) Scope boundary — cutlines between commission and follow-up, 'should we also do X?' questions; (4) Shape of persisted or inter-component data — typed vs opaque, required vs optional, configured vs convention, when other components will consume the shape; (5) Component responsibility boundaries — who owns a behavior across engines/tools/apparatuses, when the decision sets a pattern for ownership. Everything else: pick (confidently or arbitrary-with-flag) + record. Uncertainty is not a surfacing signal — it's a planner bug (read more code). Derived in session 2d4de462; see related click on atlas staleness for cluster-C overrides (stale planner knowledge) which are a freshness problem, not a razor-criterion gap.

### c-mo1yb8nf-4dc57032ce48 [concluded]
> Codifiable policy defaults from override analysis
Status: concluded
Conclusion: Three rules, accounting for ~40% of observed overrides (clusters A+B from the override analysis). These are Sean's architectural taste expressed as standing rules, to be codified in the Astrolabe sage-writer instructions. (1) Prefer removal to deprecation — when refactoring, rip out the old path; no deprecation windows unless patron explicitly asks. (2) Prefer fail-loud to silent fallback — throw on missing input; no defaults-when-absent unless the absent case is a legitimate state. (3) Extend the API at the right layer; don't route around it — if the recommendation involves a workaround or 'the anima handles it via prompt,' default to adding the method/tool instead. Next operational step: update sage-writer instructions directly (not a commission). Derived in session 2d4de462.

### c-mo1yb8zb-d15093532db1 [concluded]
> Empirical patron-override rate baseline on Astrolabe planner output
Status: concluded
Conclusion: Baseline measurement as of 2026-04-16: ~3.7% override rate (35 overrides / ~945 total decisions across 38 specs). 24 specs (63%) had zero overrides; 14 (37%) had at least one. Hot spots: writ-parentchild-relationships (5), astrolabe-mvp-part-1 (4), parlour-page-for-oculus (4), configurable-rig-templates-via-guild (4). The planner is already correct on the bulk of decisions when a codebase pattern, architecture spec, or clear technical constraint exists. Future work targets the narrow exception path, not bulk quality. Re-measure after razor + codified defaults ship to evaluate effect. Mining done via yq extraction of patron_override fields from specs/*/decisions.yaml in session 2d4de462.

Status: concluded
Conclusion: Reconcile-engine via LLM, with stuck-escalation as fallback. On seal failure, graft a two-engine tail: (1) a quick engine where an anima attempts a rebase of the writ's changes onto the new HEAD, emitting structured success/failure output; (2) a clockwork engine that attempts push-with-ff-only-rebase. Single LLM attempt; if step 2 still can't push, the rig goes stuck. Commissioned in brief w-mo21fy79-c6e76e507be3.

### c-mo1uw15a-4377fc9722b0 [concluded]
> Does the planner have better information than the implementer?
Status: concluded
Conclusion: No. Both read the same codebase. The planner's enumeration of files, consumers, etc. is no more reliable than the implementer's. Presenting planner enumerations as authoritative actively suppresses the implementer's audit instinct. Name the concern and verification method instead — let the implementer discover the actual set. Decision taken in design session 860f69d7. Original quest body: docs/archive/quests/w-mo0v636y-41c8aeff857f.json.

### c-mo1uw22y-9129aa0a62e8 [concluded]
> How should planner decisions be tiered and resolved?
Status: concluded
Conclusion: Four tiers: (1) Hardcoded — obviously correct, no variance expected; (2) Configurable preference — set once, not actively learned; (3) Pluggable + measurable — system picks winner via outcome data; (4) Pluggable + value-gated — requires patron signal. Most decisions just proceed: Tiers 1–3 are made, recorded, and moved forward. Only Tier 4 surfaces for ratification, and even then the system has already proceeded with a default. Decision taken in design session 860f69d7. Original quest body: docs/archive/quests/w-mo0v636y-41c8aeff857f.json.

Conclusion: Reconcile-engine via LLM, with stuck-escalation as fallback. On seal failure, graft a two-engine tail: (1) a quick engine where an anima attempts a rebase of the writ's changes onto the new HEAD, emitting structured success/failure output; (2) a clockwork engine that attempts push-with-ff-only-rebase. Single LLM attempt; if step 2 still can't push, the rig goes stuck. Commissioned in brief w-mo21fy79-c6e76e507be3.

### c-mo2c33rv-0d22e9372938 [concluded]
> What writ link types are already in use across the guild?
Status: concluded
Conclusion: 100 links in use across 12 distinct types (queried books_clerk_links on 2026-04-17):

  refines       30
  retries       23
  fulfilled_by  21
  depends on     9   ← space-separated
  builds-on      5
  instance_of    3
  enhances       2
  fixes          2
  relates-to     2
  depends-on     1   ← hyphenated (same semantic as "depends on")
  related        1   ← same semantic as "relates-to"
  supersedes     1

Footgun evidence realized in the data:

- Typo drift: "depends on" (9) vs "depends-on" (1) — same semantic, two spellings. String-matching either one misses ~90% of the dependency data. "blocked-by" (the name the MVP click assumes) doesn't appear at all.
- Overlapping synonyms: relates-to/related; refines/builds-on/enhances — taxonomy drift, no canonical form.
- Three separator conventions coexist: space ("depends on"), hyphen ("depends-on", "relates-to", "builds-on"), underscore ("fulfilled_by", "instance_of"). No convention enforcement.

Conclusion: the open-string type field has already produced inconsistent usage at the 100-link scale. Any mechanism that relies on Spider/consumer code string-matching specific type names will have silent misses on existing data unless a canonicalization pass or a reserved-name enforcement mechanism is adopted.
Created: 2026-04-17T03:13:38.779Z
Resolved: 2026-04-17T03:13:47.949Z

### c-mo2c3bs8-a876bbc50364 [concluded]
> Choose the mechanism for load-bearing writ links (codified open-string vs reserved-name registry vs structural field vs registered-enum)
Status: concluded
Conclusion: Chosen mechanism: the (type, semanticMeaning?) split. Writ links carry a human-readable `type` field (the label, kept as-is from today's schema) and an optional `semanticMeaning` field that references a plugin-owned registered meaning. See child click conclusions for parameter decisions:

- c-mo2db4n7 — split design (label + optional contract reference)
- c-mo2dbdnw — meaning assignment is EXPLICIT (author declares, never inferred)
- c-mo2dbd3d — pairing policy is OPEN (no name-meaning validation)
- c-mo2dbea3 — normalization: lowercase space-delimited canonical form
- c-mo2dbey3 — polarity lives in the meaning schema; names carry no direction metadata

Implementation direction established in session:
- Keep existing `WritLinkDoc.type` field (no rename) — its role shifts from "structural type" to "human-readable label"; normalization applied in place
- Add optional `semanticMeaning?: string` field (id reference) to WritLinkDoc
- Clerk owns the meaning registry; meanings are declared as plugin-manifest kit contributions
- Meaning id format: plugin-qualified with `.` separator (e.g. `spider.precedence-gate`), matching the existing role id convention
- Meaning registry schema is minimal: `{id, ownerPlugin, description}` — direction/cardinality/hooks deferred until a second consumer or cross-plugin coordination creates actual demand

Brief forthcoming for the substrate work (schema change + normalization + registry primitive + migration). Declaring specific meanings (e.g. `spider.precedence-gate`) and wiring Spider to consume them are separate briefs.
Created: 2026-04-17T03:13:49.161Z
Resolved: 2026-04-17T04:05:31.661Z

#### c-mo2db4n7-074b97bc036c [concluded]
> Should a writ link's type be a single attribute or split into label + contract?
Status: concluded
Conclusion: Split into two attributes on a link:

- `name` — human-readable label, arbitrary string with light normalization. Free-form; expresses what the author wants to say. Consumer code does NOT bind to name.
- `semanticMeaning?` — optional reference (id) into a plugin-owned registry of registered meanings. Each meaning carries direction, cardinality, and policy hooks. Consumer code (e.g. Spider) binds to semanticMeaning.

Key properties of the split:
- Multiple distinct names may map to the same meaning (synonymy is first-class: "depends on", "blocked by", "requires" can all resolve to the same `precedence-gate` meaning).
- Links with meaning=null are documentary only — no policy, no machine behavior.
- Policy/hooks live in the registry entry, referenced by id, so meaning semantics can evolve without rewriting existing links.
- Migration of existing data is zero-loss: current `type` field becomes `name`, meaning starts null, and meanings are assigned later where load-bearing.

Sub-decisions (pairing policy, assignment policy, normalization rules, polarity representation) remain open — see sibling clicks.
Created: 2026-04-17T03:47:52.771Z
Resolved: 2026-04-17T03:48:02.543Z

#### c-mo2dbd3d-83b7ce2cabfc [concluded]
> Should the name↔meaning pairing be a closed list (reject unregistered combos), open with warnings (allow but flag drift), or tiered (hard for polarity/cross-meaning misuse, soft for novel names)?
Status: concluded
Conclusion: Open. Any name may be paired with any `semanticMeaning`. No validation or warning on name↔meaning pairings at link creation.

Rationale:
- Closed list (reject non-canonical pairings): drawbacks too high — blocks emergent synonymy for existing meanings, forces ceremony on edge cases, imposes taxonomic decisions up front without empirical data, and creates a migration tax on novel expression.
- Tiered (hard in some cases, soft in others): confusing — different rules per dimension (polarity vs cross-meaning vs novel name) creates a policy surface that authors and reviewers have to hold in their heads.
- Warnings-as-logs (allow with a logged warning): not actually load-bearing. Warning channels aren't surfaced to operators in practice, so building the validation infrastructure buys nothing real — the warnings just get swallowed.

The open approach accepts one cost: authors can pair nonsensical name+meaning combos (e.g. `{name: "fixes", meaning: precedence-gate}`). Operationally harmless — Spider binds to meaning, not name, so the system behaves correctly. Humans reading the graph may occasionally be confused by a mismatched name/meaning. That confusion is absorbed rather than prevented.

Implementation implication: the plugin-owned meaning registry tracks meanings only (direction, cardinality, hooks). No per-meaning canonical-names list is maintained. Name validation is purely normalization (see sibling click on normalization rules). Pairing is never rejected.
Created: 2026-04-17T03:48:03.721Z
Resolved: 2026-04-17T03:54:12.400Z

#### c-mo2dbdnw-0aa12dfdadec [concluded]
> How is semanticMeaning assigned on a link — author-declared explicit, inferred from name, or hybrid?
Status: concluded
Conclusion: Explicit. The author declares `semanticMeaning` at link creation time; the system never infers it from the name.

Rationale: Auto-inference (deriving meaning from a canonical name) creates silent semantic attribution — an author using a name like "depends on" for documentary purposes would have `precedence-gate` meaning attached without intending it, and the Spider would start gating dispatch on a link the author thought was a note. Explicit declaration keeps documentary use documentary and load-bearing use deliberate.

Implementation implication: the link-creation API accepts an optional `--meaning` / `semanticMeaning` parameter; omitting it creates a documentary (meaning=null) link. Authors who intend a load-bearing relationship must know the meaning registry and pass the meaning id explicitly. CLI affordances (e.g. `nsg writ link-meanings` to discover registered meanings) are a tooling concern, not part of this decision.

Trade accepted: authors can forget to attach meaning and silently produce a documentary link where they intended a load-bearing one. No machine-level protection against that — by design, the system prefers under-attribution to over-attribution.
Created: 2026-04-17T03:48:04.460Z
Resolved: 2026-04-17T03:54:04.238Z

#### c-mo2dbea3-3c72ffec110b [concluded]
> What name normalization rules apply before registry lookup (case, whitespace, separators, synonyms)?
Status: concluded
Conclusion: Names are normalized to lowercase space-delimited form at link creation. Canonical stored form is the result of applying the following transforms in order:

1. Lowercase (e.g. "Depends On" → "depends on")
2. Trim leading and trailing whitespace
3. Convert camelCase to space-delimited (e.g. "dependsOn" → "depends on")
4. Convert snake_case to space-delimited (e.g. "depends_on" → "depends on")
5. Convert kebab-case to space-delimited (e.g. "depends-on" → "depends on")
6. Collapse multiple internal whitespace characters to a single space

Result: "depends on", "depends-on", "depends_on", "dependsOn", "Depends On" all normalize to the canonical form "depends on".

Rationale: pick the most natural-language form as canonical. "depends on" dominates in existing data (9 uses vs 1 for "depends-on"), is the most human-readable, and makes normalized names look like prose. Four separator conventions in current use (space / hyphen / underscore / camelCase) all collapse to one.

Normalization is purely syntactic — no synonymy mapping. "depends on" and "requires" remain distinct names; if they should resolve to the same behavior, that happens at the meaning layer (both linked to `precedence-gate`), not at normalization.

Implementation: normalization applied at insert time and on query. Stored values are always canonical. Querying by name applies the same transform before lookup.
Created: 2026-04-17T03:48:05.259Z
Resolved: 2026-04-17T03:54:19.243Z

#### c-mo2dbey3-b8e497ebd8c8 [concluded]
> How is polarity/direction represented for names that reverse a meaning's canonical direction (e.g., 'blocks' vs 'blocked by')?
Status: concluded
Conclusion: Polarity lives in the semanticMeaning schema alone. Each registered meaning carries a canonical direction (e.g. precedence-gate: source writ is blocked until target writ completes). Names themselves carry no polarity metadata — consistent with the open pairing policy (names are free labels, not registry entries).

Authors control edge direction by choosing which writ is source and which is target, and pick whatever name reads naturally for that direction. If the author wants "A blocks B" semantics (B is gated by A), they create the edge as B→A with any fitting name ("blocks", "blocked by", "requires", etc.). The system interprets based on the meaning's canonical direction; the name is purely human-facing.

This dissolves the name-direction mismatch problem (e.g. "blocks" vs "blocked by" reading as the same concept but opposite polarity) — the system simply doesn't look at names for direction; direction is a property of the meaning.
Created: 2026-04-17T03:48:06.124Z
Resolved: 2026-04-17T04:05:23.158Z

### c-mo2e87dy-172194b5ad53 [concluded]
> Declare the spider.precedence-gate meaning — exact description, polarity convention, and the kit contribution that registers it
Status: concluded
Conclusion: Meaning id: **spider.dependency** (shortened from the working name spider.precedence-gate). Owner: spider plugin.

Description: "Source writ cannot dispatch until the target writ reaches `completed`." Gate releases only on `completed` — not on any terminal state. A failed or cancelled blocker does not release the dependent, because the thing the dependent was waiting on was never delivered.

Polarity: edge source→target. Source is gated; target is the prerequisite. Names on the link are free-form labels (e.g. "depends on"); the meaning carries the direction.

Failure handling: when a blocker reaches `failed` or `cancelled`, Spider transitions the dependent to `stuck` with a stuck-reason naming the blocker writ. Visibility over silence; reversible if the blocker is retried and completes. Chosen over silent-undispatchable (accretes zombie writs) and cascade-fail (destructive and worse for recovery).

Declaration packaging: the `supportKit.linkMeanings` entry is added as part of the c-mo2e88aw Spider-wiring commission, not a standalone commission — it's a ~5-line manifest addition with no standalone value.

Followup: retry ergonomics spun as a sibling click (see below) — the mechanical recovery path (repost blocker, relink dependent, unlink old) is clunky and is a symptom of the missing retry primitive, not a problem with the gate semantic.
Created: 2026-04-17T04:13:35.974Z
Resolved: 2026-04-17T13:55:18.436Z

### c-mo2e88aw-f4d5684cf385 [concluded]
> Wire Spider to gate dispatch on inbound spider.precedence-gate links — scheduler integration, failed/cancelled-blocker propagation, operator visibility
Status: concluded
Conclusion: Design fully resolved and dispatched as commission w-mo35s0fo-1a1e3cd285bc.

Final shape: Spider gates dispatch on inbound spider.follows links (conjunctive composition, polling re-evaluation, cycle detection during gate walk, auto-unstick on recovery, provenance via status.spider slot). Two substrate adjustments land alongside: (1) status convention for runtime objects (K8s-style spec/status, plugin-namespaced slots, see c-mo33duvq); (2) link-substrate naming sweep (type→label, semanticMeaning→kind, linkMeanings→linkKinds, separator colon→dot per the system-wide convention at c-mo34644p; see c-mo34jdht). Authoring surfaces (CLI + the existing inline add-link form on the writs page) extended to expose both label and kind. Visibility-gap concern (open status overload) parked separately at c-mo301yp9 for future Oculus work.
Created: 2026-04-17T04:13:37.160Z
Resolved: 2026-04-17T17:05:43.545Z

#### c-mo2zim46-8a9e69f209f3 [concluded]
> Lock the spider.follows meaning's final name and description text for the kit contribution
Status: concluded
Conclusion: Name: **spider.follows**. Owner plugin: spider.

Description (verbatim for the kit contribution):

"The source writ is a precedence-successor of the target: source cannot be dispatched until the target reaches a terminal state. Consumers define their own policy for what happens on each terminal state."

Kit-contribution shape:

```typescript
supportKit: {
  linkMeanings: [
    {
      id: 'spider.follows',
      description: 'The source writ is a precedence-successor of the target: source cannot be dispatched until the target reaches a terminal state. Consumers define their own policy for what happens on each terminal state.',
    },
  ],
},
```

Rationale for choices:
- **`spider.follows`** over `spider.dependency` (implies success-coupling, which the meaning does not), `spider.precedence` (reads direction backwards when paired with source→target convention), or `spider.sequence` (noun for a list, not a relationship). `follows` is a directional verb that reads naturally as source→target ("A follows B" means A comes after B), same 7-char length as `sequence`, unambiguous.
- **Terminal-state gate release** with per-consumer policy on individual terminal states — separates the ordering primitive from behavior. Keeps the meaning a pure temporal-ordering contract; Spider's specific stuck-on-failure behavior is its policy, not part of the meaning.

This supersedes the stale naming (`spider.dependency`) and description in c-mo2e87dy's conclusion — that click was concluded before these refinements landed.

Conclusion: Polarity lives in the semanticMeaning schema alone. Each registered meaning carries a canonical direction (e.g. precedence-gate: source writ is blocked until target writ completes). Names themselves carry no polarity metadata — consistent with the open pairing policy (names are free labels, not registry entries).

Authors control edge direction by choosing which writ is source and which is target, and pick whatever name reads naturally for that direction. If the author wants "A blocks B" semantics (B is gated by A), they create the edge as B→A with any fitting name ("blocks", "blocked by", "requires", etc.). The system interprets based on the meaning's canonical direction; the name is purely human-facing.

This dissolves the name-direction mismatch problem (e.g. "blocks" vs "blocked by" reading as the same concept but opposite polarity) — the system simply doesn't look at names for direction; direction is a property of the meaning.
Created: 2026-04-17T03:48:06.124Z
Resolved: 2026-04-17T04:05:23.158Z

### c-mo2e87dy-172194b5ad53 [concluded]
> Declare the spider.precedence-gate meaning — exact description, polarity convention, and the kit contribution that registers it
Status: concluded
Conclusion: Meaning id: **spider.dependency** (shortened from the working name spider.precedence-gate). Owner: spider plugin.

Description: "Source writ cannot dispatch until the target writ reaches `completed`." Gate releases only on `completed` — not on any terminal state. A failed or cancelled blocker does not release the dependent, because the thing the dependent was waiting on was never delivered.

Polarity: edge source→target. Source is gated; target is the prerequisite. Names on the link are free-form labels (e.g. "depends on"); the meaning carries the direction.

Failure handling: when a blocker reaches `failed` or `cancelled`, Spider transitions the dependent to `stuck` with a stuck-reason naming the blocker writ. Visibility over silence; reversible if the blocker is retried and completes. Chosen over silent-undispatchable (accretes zombie writs) and cascade-fail (destructive and worse for recovery).

Declaration packaging: the `supportKit.linkMeanings` entry is added as part of the c-mo2e88aw Spider-wiring commission, not a standalone commission — it's a ~5-line manifest addition with no standalone value.

Followup: retry ergonomics spun as a sibling click (see below) — the mechanical recovery path (repost blocker, relink dependent, unlink old) is clunky and is a symptom of the missing retry primitive, not a problem with the gate semantic.
Created: 2026-04-17T04:13:35.974Z
Resolved: 2026-04-17T13:55:18.436Z

### c-mo2e88aw-f4d5684cf385 [concluded]
> Wire Spider to gate dispatch on inbound spider.precedence-gate links — scheduler integration, failed/cancelled-blocker propagation, operator visibility
Status: concluded
Conclusion: Design fully resolved and dispatched as commission w-mo35s0fo-1a1e3cd285bc.

Final shape: Spider gates dispatch on inbound spider.follows links (conjunctive composition, polling re-evaluation, cycle detection during gate walk, auto-unstick on recovery, provenance via status.spider slot). Two substrate adjustments land alongside: (1) status convention for runtime objects (K8s-style spec/status, plugin-namespaced slots, see c-mo33duvq); (2) link-substrate naming sweep (type→label, semanticMeaning→kind, linkMeanings→linkKinds, separator colon→dot per the system-wide convention at c-mo34644p; see c-mo34jdht). Authoring surfaces (CLI + the existing inline add-link form on the writs page) extended to expose both label and kind. Visibility-gap concern (open status overload) parked separately at c-mo301yp9 for future Oculus work.
Created: 2026-04-17T04:13:37.160Z
Resolved: 2026-04-17T17:05:43.545Z

#### c-mo2zim46-8a9e69f209f3 [concluded]
> Lock the spider.follows meaning's final name and description text for the kit contribution
Status: concluded
Conclusion: Name: **spider.follows**. Owner plugin: spider.

Description (verbatim for the kit contribution):

"The source writ is a precedence-successor of the target: source cannot be dispatched until the target reaches a terminal state. Consumers define their own policy for what happens on each terminal state."

Kit-contribution shape:

```typescript
supportKit: {
  linkMeanings: [
    {
      id: 'spider.follows',
      description: 'The source writ is a precedence-successor of the target: source cannot be dispatched until the target reaches a terminal state. Consumers define their own policy for what happens on each terminal state.',
    },
  ],
},
```

Rationale for choices:
- **`spider.follows`** over `spider.dependency` (implies success-coupling, which the meaning does not), `spider.precedence` (reads direction backwards when paired with source→target convention), or `spider.sequence` (noun for a list, not a relationship). `follows` is a directional verb that reads naturally as source→target ("A follows B" means A comes after B), same 7-char length as `sequence`, unambiguous.
- **Terminal-state gate release** with per-consumer policy on individual terminal states — separates the ordering primitive from behavior. Keeps the meaning a pure temporal-ordering contract; Spider's specific stuck-on-failure behavior is its policy, not part of the meaning.

This supersedes the stale naming (`spider.dependency`) and description in c-mo2e87dy's conclusion — that click was concluded before these refinements landed.

input.description: See parent relationships for serial hopper

lastPrompt: where are we at with our autonomous operation work? whats the next best step?

content.text:
Hey Sean — here's where we stand on the autonomous operation push.

## Context

The umbrella click is **"Unlock autonomous hopper-based operation for Nexus"** (c-mo1mq8ry). Under it, the concrete MVP milestone is **autonomous-but-serial hopper** (`maxConcurrentEngines: 1`) — get one commission at a time flowing end-to-end without human intervention before worrying about concurrency.

The serial-hopper milestone has 5 design pillars. Status today:

| # | Pillar | Status |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Load-bearing `blocked_by` links** (scheduler prerequisites) | ✅ Designed → **dispatched** as `w-mo35s0fo` ("Spider Dispatch Gating via `spider.follows`") — in flight right now |
| 2 | **Failure & retry policy** for autonomous commissions | Undesigned (retry-ergonomics subthread already surfaced under pillar 1) |
| 3 | **Stuck-rig detection & recovery** (timeouts + reaper) | Undesigned |
| 4 | **Queue observability** (what's running/queued/stuck/parked) | Undesigned |
| 5 | **Intake tier model** (backlog / ready pool / parked) | Undesigned |

Parallel threads that are also live and relevant:
- **Planner→implementer fidelity gap** (c-mo21amsd) — recent sage-writer pivot to inlined click content is chipping at this from the other side.
- **Seal-engine rebase recovery** (c-mo1mqb0r) — design closed, commissioned as `w-mo21fy79`. Reconcile-engine plus stuck-escalation.
- **"Why doesn't the reviewer reject?"** (c-mo1mqbnr) — still an open question, not a gating concern for serial MVP.

Commission log has **250 entries with null complexity** — large backlog. Not worth trying to backfill; we should start rating new ones going forward and let old ones stay as-is.

## Options for the next best step

1. **Wait on `w-mo35s0fo`, then design retry policy (pillar 2).** Retry is the most load-bearing undesigned pillar — stuck-rig recovery and failure handling both interleave with it, and a retry-ergonomics tangent already surfaced while designing `spider.follows`. Designing retry first lets pillars 3 and 4 lean on it.

2. **Design retry + stuck-rig together (pillars 2 & 3 as one session).** They're the same question from two angles ("what happens when a rig doesn't complete cleanly?"). A combined design session might be more coherent than splitting them.

3. **Design queue observability (pillar 4) first.** Ops-surface first so you can actually *watch* autonomous flow when we turn it on. Less foundational but highest "can I tell what the system is doing" payoff.

4. **Intake tier model (pillar 5).** Probably lowest priority for serial MVP — with `maxConcurrentEngines: 1` the hopper naturally paces itself. More valuable once we go concurrent.

5. **Switch tracks to planner→implementer fidelity.** Recent sage-writer pivot is mid-flight; could follow through on that thread while the Spider commission lands.

## Recommendation

**Option 2** — design retry + stuck-rig as a paired session once `w-mo35s0fo` lands (or, if you're impatient, start now; it's mostly orthogonal work). They're genuinely the same conversation, and together they define the "what happens when things go sideways" contract that everything else in the hopper has to respect.

## Next steps

- Confirm which pillar (or pair) to tackle next.
- I'll pull up the relevant clicks, do some pre-session exploration (enumerate failure modes, sketch the decision surface), and we go from there.
- Meanwhile, `w-mo35s0fo` is running — worth checking in on when we want to review it.

Want to dig into retry/stuck-rig, or is there a different thread calling louder?

content: We're unblocking autonomous serial-hopper operation; the `spider.follows` dispatch-gating commission (`w-mo35s0fo`) is in flight, closing one of five MVP pillars. Next: pick the next pillar to design, recommended retry + stuck-rig recovery as a paired session. (disable recaps in /config)

content.content:
{
  "id": "w-mo35s0fo-1a1e3cd285bc",
  "type": "brief",
  "status": "open",
  "title": "Spider Dispatch Gating via `spider.follows`",
  "body": "# Spider Dispatch Gating via `spider.follows`

## Intent

Wire the Spider so that writ dispatch can be gated on inbound links of a load-bearing kind that expresses precedence. A writ that depends on others is held until those others reach a terminal state; success releases the gate, non-success terminal states put the dependent into `stuck`. Recovery (a previously failed dependency eventually succeeding) auto-unsticks the dependent. Cycles in the dependency graph are detected by Spider and surfaced as a stuck condition.

This commission is the first real consumer of the link-meaning substrate. Alongside the Spider gating work, it tightens two pieces of substrate that this consumer pressure-tests:

- **Status convention** for runtime objects (Kubernetes-style spec/status split). Spider needs a place to record its own observed state and the provenance of stuck transitions. This first slot is named here; the convention generalizes to other plugins.
- **Naming sweep on the link-meaning substrate** — its current field names actively mislead readers and its id format is inconsistent with the rest of the system. This commission lands the corrections.

## Motivation

The link-meaning substrate is in place but unused. No plugin reacts to a meaning yet. The Spider currently dispatches every `open` writ of a dispatchable type as soon as concurrency allows, with no awareness of relationships between writs. Any real workflow with ordering (build before deploy, parent before child wrap-up, etc.) has to rely on the operator authoring writs in a specific sequence and never benefits from the substrate that exists for exactly this purpose.

Spider is the natural first consumer because it already owns the dispatch decision. The questions that needed answering — composition, re-evaluation mechanism, cycle handling, recovery semantics, provenance — were worked through in the click subtree at `c-mo2e88aw-f4d5684cf385`.

## Non-negotiable decisions

### The kind: `spider.follows`

A new link-kind contributed by the Spider plugin.

**Description (verbatim):** *"The source writ is a precedence-successor of the target: source cannot be dispatched until the target reaches a terminal state. Consumers define their own policy for what happens on each terminal state."*

The kind is a pure temporal-ordering contract. Spider's specific policy (stuck-on-failure, auto-unstick on recovery) is its own consumer-side behavior, not part of the kind's meaning. A future consumer with different recovery semantics could legitimately bind to the same kind.

### Composition: conjunctive

Multiple inbound `spider.follows` links are conjunctive — *all* must release before the dependent dispatches. Any single non-terminal blocker keeps the gate closed; any single failed blocker puts the dependent into `stuck`.

### Re-evaluation: Spider's poll

Gate state is re-evaluated on Spider's existing crawl loop. No new event-dispatch substrate is required. The same poll that picks up newly-eligible writs also re-walks the dependencies of currently-gated and currently-stuck writs.

### Cycle handling: Spider detects, Clerk stays thin

Cycles in the `spider.follows` graph are detected by Spider during gate evaluation, not prevented by Clerk at link-creation. Clerk remains link-kind-agnostic. A cycle puts every writ in the cycle into `stuck` with a clear cycle-related reason; cycles are recoverable (when one member transitions out via external action, the others auto-unstick on the next poll).

### Auto-unstick on recovery

When a previously-failed blocker eventually reaches success (typically via operator-driven retry), Spider auto-unsticks the gated dependent on the next poll, returning it to `open`. Rationale: the operator's action *was* fixing the dependency; requiring a second manual unstick step is redundant ceremony. The gated state is purely derived from blocker state; once the cause is resolved, the symptom is resolved.

### Provenance: Spider records its own causes

Spider only auto-unsticks writs whose stuck transition Spider itself authored. The mechanism is the new status convention (see below): Spider records `stuckCause` and supporting detail in its own status slot. This cleanly separates Spider-authored stucks from stucks caused by other actors (operator, future plugins, engine cascade), which Spider will not touch.

### Authoring surfaces expose both `label` and `kind`

Wherever a link can be created interactively, both fields are first-class inputs with the right control type for each:

- **CLI** (`nsg writ link …`) accepts `label` as a positional/named argument and `kind` as an optional flag. Both are documented in the command's help text. (The existing command already supports the equivalent shape under the old names; this commission carries the rename through and tightens the help text.)
- **Oculus writ page** has an inline add-link form on each writ. Today it exposes only the casual label (under the old name); this commission completes it: `label` stays as a free text input (its existing autocomplete datalist is preserved), and a new `kind` control is added as a dropdown sourced from the kit-contributed registry (`listKinds()`). Operators don't type kind ids by hand. The unlink controls and link-row rendering rename in lockstep with the underlying field.

Authoring with `kind` omitted creates a documentary link (`kind = null`); this is the casual-tagging path and remains supported on every authoring surface.

### Stuck reason text

Operator-facing `resolution` text on a Spider-stuck writ is human-readable and names the specific blockers by short id:

- *"Blocked by failed dependency: w-abc123"*
- *"Blocked by failed dependencies: w-abc123, w-def456"* (multiple)
- *"Cycle detected in spider.follows graph"*

The structured detail (which blockers, which states, when observed) lives in the Spider status slot — `resolution` is the human summary; status is the machine-readable truth.

### Lifecycle of a gated writ

Gating happens entirely from `open`. The `new` status means draft (author still amending) and is out of scope for Spider gating.

| Transition | Trigger |
|---|---|
| `new` (draft) — out of scope | Author amending; Spider does not touch. |
| `open` (gated) — Spider sees, skips dispatch | Inbound `spider.follows` blocker is non-terminal. Spider records waiting-state in its status slot. |
| `open` → dispatched | All blockers reached success terminal state. |
| `open` → `stuck` (failed dependency) | A blocker reached a non-success terminal state (failed/cancelled). |
| `open` → `stuck` (cycle) | Spider detected a cycle during gate-graph walk. |
| `stuck` → `open` (auto-unstick) | All previously-failed blockers eventually reached success (or cycle resolved). |

A known visibility gap: `open` now overloads "finalized-and-waiting", "finalized-and-eligible", and "actively-running". Captured separately at `c-mo301yp9` (parked) for future surfacing work — likely an Oculus column reading from the Spider status slot.

## Substrate changes

Two substrate adjustments land alongside the Spider work. Both are informed and motivated by Spider being the first real consumer; the substrate as it exists today carries decisions made before any consumer pressure-tested it.

### Status convention for runtime objects

Adopt a Kubernetes-style spec/status split for runtime objects. Each runtime object carries a per-plugin status namespace; each plugin owns the slot at `status.<pluginId>` and writes only its own slot. Other plugins (and Oculus) read freely. The convention is enforced by convention only — no runtime permission system in v1.

Decisions that apply to the convention (full design at `c-mo33duvq-7f774446c6c9`):

- **Ownership** — convention only, not enforced.
- **Change events** — status mutations emit Stacks events the same as any other field mutation. Watchers can react to status changes.
- **Persistence** — status persists for the lifetime of the object, including across terminal states.
- **Concurrent writes** — last-writer-wins per key, no merge semantics. Plugins serialize their own writes if they need stronger semantics.
- **Typed contributions (deferred)** — eventually plugins may declare their status shape via kit contribution for type-checking. Not required in v1.

In this commission, the convention is added to writs only — Spider is the only consumer. The convention is documented as guild-wide; other runtime objects (rigs, engines, sessions, etc.) adopt it when their first consumer needs the slot.

### Link-kind substrate naming sweep

The link-meaning substrate landed before any consumer used it. Two naming choices need correcting before the substrate has a real consumer.

**Field renames on the link record:**

| Old | New | Why |
|---|---|---|
| `type` | `label` | "type" misleads readers into thinking the field is load-bearing; it is explicitly not. "label" signals display/tagging. |
| `semanticMeaning` | `kind` | "semanticMeaning" is a mouthful; "kind" is the conventional name for a load-bearing classification id (consistent with Kubernetes' `kind` field, which the status convention also references). |

**Vocabulary sweep across the substrate** for consistency:

| Old | New |
|---|---|
| `linkMeanings` (kit field) | `linkKinds` |
| `MeaningDoc` | `KindDoc` |
| `listMeanings()` | `listKinds()` |
| `MeaningEntry` and other related names | `KindEntry`, etc. |
| All prose, docstrings, README examples | Updated in lockstep |

**Id separator: colon → dot.** Plugin-namespaced identifiers across the system (role contributions like `astrolabe.plan-init`, event patterns like `writ.created`, etc.) use dots. The link-kind substrate using colons (`astrolabe:refines`) was the outlier. Convention captured at `c-mo34644p`. After this commission, all plugin-namespaced ids — including link-kind ids — use the dot form.

## Out of scope

- **Database / data migration of existing data.** Handled out of band by the patron. The brief assumes the implementing artificer can do an in-place rename across code and docs without simultaneously migrating live records.
- **New CLI command for `spider.follows`.** The existing `nsg writ link` (post-rename) is the authoring surface — no `spider`-specific command. The CLI's `label`/`kind` exposure is in scope (see "Authoring surfaces" above) but no new commands are introduced.
- **A richer link-authoring UI in Oculus** (writ picker with search, kind-aware suggestions, etc.). The existing inline add-link form on the writ page is extended in scope (see "Authoring surfaces"), but no broader UX redesign.
- **Oculus rendering of Spider's status slot.** A natural next step (gate column on the writ table, etc.) but a separate commission.
- **Status convention for non-writ runtime objects.** Status is added to writs only in this commission. Other runtime objects adopt it when their first consumer needs it.
- **Ownership enforcement of status slots** at runtime. Convention only in v1.
- **Typed contributions for plugin status shapes.** Deferred (parked at `c-mo33e194`).
- **Closing the open-status visibility gap.** Captured at `c-mo301yp9` (parked).
- **Retroactive cycle scan of existing data.** No existing links are `spider.follows`; a scan is moot.
- **Hopper concurrency interactions.** Gate evaluation is upstream of concurrency; no change to existing concurrency limits.
- **Anything that depends on Clockworks / standing orders.** The polling decision is deliberate; this commission does not introduce event-dispatch substrate.

## References

- **Design subtree** (this commission): click `c-mo2e88aw-f4d5684cf385` and its concluded children.
- **Status convention** design: click `c-mo33duvq-7f774446c6c9` and its five concluded children (one parked: typed contributions).
- **System-wide separator convention** (dots for plugin-namespaced ids): click `c-mo34644p-2aebcbb478bb`.
- **Link-substrate naming sweep** (label, kind, vocabulary): click `c-mo34jdht-c9bbe8fe43f3`.
- **Final kind naming** (`spider.follows`): click `c-mo2zim46-8a9e69f209f3` (name + description), with the id-format adjustment captured at `c-mo3465sf-d9187bc9322a`.
- **Visibility-gap follow-up** (parked): click `c-mo301yp9-c186db746a77`.
- **Prior substrate brief** (introduces the link-meaning layer this commission consumes and renames): `.scratch/brief-writ-link-meaning-substrate.md`.",
  "codex": "nexus",
  "createdAt": "2026-04-17T17:04:49.716Z",
  "updatedAt": "2026-04-17T17:04:49.716Z",
  "links": {
    "outbound": [],
    "inbound": []
  },
  "parent": null,
  "children": {
    "summary": {},

  {
    "id": "w-mo35s0fo-1a1e3cd285bc",
    "type": "brief",
    "status": "open",
    "title": "Spider Dispatch Gating via `spider.follows`",
    "body": "# Spider Dispatch Gating via `spider.follows`

## Intent

Wire the Spider so that writ dispatch can be gated on inbound links of a load-bearing kind that expresses precedence. A writ that depends on others is held until those others reach a terminal state; success releases the gate, non-success terminal states put the dependent into `stuck`. Recovery (a previously failed dependency eventually succeeding) auto-unsticks the dependent. Cycles in the dependency graph are detected by Spider and surfaced as a stuck condition.

This commission is the first real consumer of the link-meaning substrate. Alongside the Spider gating work, it tightens two pieces of substrate that this consumer pressure-tests:

- **Status convention** for runtime objects (Kubernetes-style spec/status split). Spider needs a place to record its own observed state and the provenance of stuck transitions. This first slot is named here; the convention generalizes to other plugins.
- **Naming sweep on the link-meaning substrate** — its current field names actively mislead readers and its id format is inconsistent with the rest of the system. This commission lands the corrections.

## Motivation

The link-meaning substrate is in place but unused. No plugin reacts to a meaning yet. The Spider currently dispatches every `open` writ of a dispatchable type as soon as concurrency allows, with no awareness of relationships between writs. Any real workflow with ordering (build before deploy, parent before child wrap-up, etc.) has to rely on the operator authoring writs in a specific sequence and never benefits from the substrate that exists for exactly this purpose.

Spider is the natural first consumer because it already owns the dispatch decision. The questions that needed answering — composition, re-evaluation mechanism, cycle handling, recovery semantics, provenance — were worked through in the click subtree at `c-mo2e88aw-f4d5684cf385`.

## Non-negotiable decisions

### The kind: `spider.follows`

A new link-kind contributed by the Spider plugin.

**Description (verbatim):** *"The source writ is a precedence-successor of the target: source cannot be dispatched until the target reaches a terminal state. Consumers define their own policy for what happens on each terminal state."*

The kind is a pure temporal-ordering contract. Spider's specific policy (stuck-on-failure, auto-unstick on recovery) is its own consumer-side behavior, not part of the kind's meaning. A future consumer with different recovery semantics could legitimately bind to the same kind.

### Composition: conjunctive

Multiple inbound `spider.follows` links are conjunctive — *all* must release before the dependent dispatches. Any single non-terminal blocker keeps the gate closed; any single failed blocker puts the dependent into `stuck`.

### Re-evaluation: Spider's poll

Gate state is re-evaluated on Spider's existing crawl loop. No new event-dispatch substrate is required. The same poll that picks up newly-eligible writs also re-walks the dependencies of currently-gated and currently-stuck writs.

### Cycle handling: Spider detects, Clerk stays thin

Cycles in the `spider.follows` graph are detected by Spider during gate evaluation, not prevented by Clerk at link-creation. Clerk remains link-kind-agnostic. A cycle puts every writ in the cycle into `stuck` with a clear cycle-related reason; cycles are recoverable (when one member transitions out via external action, the others auto-unstick on the next poll).

### Auto-unstick on recovery

When a previously-failed blocker eventually reaches success (typically via operator-driven retry), Spider auto-unsticks the gated dependent on the next poll, returning it to `open`. Rationale: the operator's action *was* fixing the dependency; requiring a second manual unstick step is redundant ceremony. The gated state is purely derived from blocker state; once the cause is resolved, the symptom is resolved.

### Provenance: Spider records its own causes

Spider only auto-unsticks writs whose stuck transition Spider itself authored. The mechanism is the new status convention (see below): Spider records `stuckCause` and supporting detail in its own status slot. This cleanly separates Spider-authored stucks from stucks caused by other actors (operator, future plugins, engine cascade), which Spider will not touch.

### Authoring surfaces expose both `label` and `kind`

Wherever a link can be created interactively, both fields are first-class inputs with the right control type for each:

- **CLI** (`nsg writ link …`) accepts `label` as a positional/named argument and `kind` as an optional flag. Both are documented in the command's help text. (The existing command already supports the equivalent shape under the old names; this commission carries the rename through and tightens the help text.)
- **Oculus writ page** has an inline add-link form on each writ. Today it exposes only the casual label (under the old name); this commission completes it: `label` stays as a free text input (its existing autocomplete datalist is preserved), and a new `kind` control is added as a dropdown sourced from the kit-contributed registry (`listKinds()`). Operators don't type kind ids by hand. The unlink controls and link-row rendering rename in lockstep with the underlying field.

Authoring with `kind` omitted creates a documentary link (`kind = null`); this is the casual-tagging path and remains supported on every authoring surface.

### Stuck reason text

Operator-facing `resolution` text on a Spider-stuck writ is human-readable and names the specific blockers by short id:

- *"Blocked by failed dependency: w-abc123"*
- *"Blocked by failed dependencies: w-abc123, w-def456"* (multiple)
- *"Cycle detected in spider.follows graph"*

The structured detail (which blockers, which states, when observed) lives in the Spider status slot — `resolution` is the human summary; status is the machine-readable truth.

### Lifecycle of a gated writ

Gating happens entirely from `open`. The `new` status means draft (author still amending) and is out of scope for Spider gating.

| Transition | Trigger |
|---|---|
| `new` (draft) — out of scope | Author amending; Spider does not touch. |
| `open` (gated) — Spider sees, skips dispatch | Inbound `spider.follows` blocker is non-terminal. Spider records waiting-state in its status slot. |
| `open` → dispatched | All blockers reached success terminal state. |
| `open` → `stuck` (failed dependency) | A blocker reached a non-success terminal state (failed/cancelled). |
| `open` → `stuck` (cycle) | Spider detected a cycle during gate-graph walk. |
| `stuck` → `open` (auto-unstick) | All previously-failed blockers eventually reached success (or cycle resolved). |

A known visibility gap: `open` now overloads "finalized-and-waiting", "finalized-and-eligible", and "actively-running". Captured separately at `c-mo301yp9` (parked) for future surfacing work — likely an Oculus column reading from the Spider status slot.

## Substrate changes

Two substrate adjustments land alongside the Spider work. Both are informed and motivated by Spider being the first real consumer; the substrate as it exists today carries decisions made before any consumer pressure-tested it.

### Status convention for runtime objects

Adopt a Kubernetes-style spec/status split for runtime objects. Each runtime object carries a per-plugin status namespace; each plugin owns the slot at `status.<pluginId>` and writes only its own slot. Other plugins (and Oculus) read freely. The convention is enforced by convention only — no runtime permission system in v1.

Decisions that apply to the convention (full design at `c-mo33duvq-7f774446c6c9`):

- **Ownership** — convention only, not enforced.
- **Change events** — status mutations emit Stacks events the same as any other field mutation. Watchers can react to status changes.
- **Persistence** — status persists for the lifetime of the object, including across terminal states.
- **Concurrent writes** — last-writer-wins per key, no merge semantics. Plugins serialize their own writes if they need stronger semantics.
- **Typed contributions (deferred)** — eventually plugins may declare their status shape via kit contribution for type-checking. Not required in v1.

In this commission, the convention is added to writs only — Spider is the only consumer. The convention is documented as guild-wide; other runtime objects (rigs, engines, sessions, etc.) adopt it when their first consumer needs the slot.

### Link-kind substrate naming sweep

The link-meaning substrate landed before any consumer used it. Two naming choices need correcting before the substrate has a real consumer.

**Field renames on the link record:**

| Old | New | Why |
|---|---|---|
| `type` | `label` | "type" misleads readers into thinking the field is load-bearing; it is explicitly not. "label" signals display/tagging. |
| `semanticMeaning` | `kind` | "semanticMeaning" is a mouthful; "kind" is the conventional name for a load-bearing classification id (consistent with Kubernetes' `kind` field, which the status convention also references). |

**Vocabulary sweep across the substrate** for consistency:

| Old | New |
|---|---|
| `linkMeanings` (kit field) | `linkKinds` |
| `MeaningDoc` | `KindDoc` |
| `listMeanings()` | `listKinds()` |
| `MeaningEntry` and other related names | `KindEntry`, etc. |
| All prose, docstrings, README examples | Updated in lockstep |

**Id separator: colon → dot.** Plugin-namespaced identifiers across the system (role contributions like `astrolabe.plan-init`, event patterns like `writ.created`, etc.) use dots. The link-kind substrate using colons (`astrolabe:refines`) was the outlier. Convention captured at `c-mo34644p`. After this commission, all plugin-namespaced ids — including link-kind ids — use the dot form.

## Out of scope

- **Database / data migration of existing data.** Handled out of band by the patron. The brief assumes the implementing artificer can do an in-place rename across code and docs without simultaneously migrating live records.
- **New CLI command for `spider.follows`.** The existing `nsg writ link` (post-rename) is the authoring surface — no `spider`-specific command. The CLI's `label`/`kind` exposure is in scope (see "Authoring surfaces" above) but no new commands are introduced.
- **A richer link-authoring UI in Oculus** (writ picker with search, kind-aware suggestions, etc.). The existing inline add-link form on the writ page is extended in scope (see "Authoring surfaces"), but no broader UX redesign.
- **Oculus rendering of Spider's status slot.** A natural next step (gate column on the writ table, etc.) but a separate commission.
- **Status convention for non-writ runtime objects.** Status is added to writs only in this commission. Other runtime objects adopt it when their first consumer needs it.
- **Ownership enforcement of status slots** at runtime. Convention only in v1.
- **Typed contributions for plugin status shapes.** Deferred (parked at `c-mo33e194`).
- **Closing the open-status visibility gap.** Captured at `c-mo301yp9` (parked).
- **Retroactive cycle scan of existing data.** No existing links are `spider.follows`; a scan is moot.
- **Hopper concurrency interactions.** Gate evaluation is upstream of concurrency; no change to existing concurrency limits.
- **Anything that depends on Clockworks / standing orders.** The polling decision is deliberate; this commission does not introduce event-dispatch substrate.

## References

- **Design subtree** (this commission): click `c-mo2e88aw-f4d5684cf385` and its concluded children.
- **Status convention** design: click `c-mo33duvq-7f774446c6c9` and its five concluded children (one parked: typed contributions).
- **System-wide separator convention** (dots for plugin-namespaced ids): click `c-mo34644p-2aebcbb478bb`.
- **Link-substrate naming sweep** (label, kind, vocabulary): click `c-mo34jdht-c9bbe8fe43f3`.
- **Final kind naming** (`spider.follows`): click `c-mo2zim46-8a9e69f209f3` (name + description), with the id-format adjustment captured at `c-mo3465sf-d9187bc9322a`.
- **Visibility-gap follow-up** (parked): click `c-mo301yp9-c186db746a77`.
- **Prior substrate brief** (introduces the link-meaning layer this commission consumes and renames): `.scratch/brief-writ-link-meaning-substrate.md`.",

    "title": "Remove analysis field and tag-building from astrolabe",
    "body": "# Remove `analysis` field and tag-building from astrolabe

## Intent

Remove the `DecisionAnalysis` interface and all supporting tag-building code from the astrolabe plugin. The sage-analyst and sage-reading-analyst instructions no longer populate the `analysis` metadata, so the classification axes (`category`, `observable`, `confidence`, `stakes`) and the tag-mapping code that rendered them on the patron review UI are dead weight.

## Rationale

The analysis metadata rendered as display tags on the patron review UI but never gated which decisions surfaced — the razor alone does that. Now that the sage instructions no longer write the field, the supporting code is kept "just in case" and must go. Removing it cleans the plugin's public type surface and re-aligns the plugin README and framework architecture doc with actual behavior.

## Scope & Blast Radius

This change is confined to:

- **`packages/plugins/astrolabe`** — types, Zod schema, decision-review engine, barrel export, test suite, README.
- **`docs/architecture/apparatus/astrolabe.md`** — framework architecture doc references.

Cross-cutting concerns to watch:

- **Public API narrowing.** `DecisionAnalysis` is re-exported from the plugin barrel; removing it is a breaking change to astrolabe's public type surface. A monorepo grep currently confirms zero external consumers — verify this invariant still holds at commit time by grepping the whole repo for `DecisionAnalysis`, `buildAnalysisTags`, `decisionAnalysisSchema`, and the four tag-map constant names.
- **Zod input contract for `decisions-write`.** The tool input validator currently admits an `analysis` key. Removing that key narrows the contract. Verify no caller writes `analysis:` against `decisions-write` by grepping the repo.
- **Test-fixture type-check coupling.** Every tag-asserting test sets an `analysis: { ... }` literal on a `Decision` fixture. Any residual literal after the type is removed will fail strict TypeScript compilation of the test file. Verify by running the plugin typecheck after the test-file edit.
- **Guild-wide `tags?` field on Spider question specs.** Do NOT remove. Spider's `feedback.js` UI aggregates this field into filter chips; the type is guild-wide with non-astrolabe consumers. Astrolabe must simply stop emitting tags.
- **Doc/code alignment.** The plugin README's `Decision` + `DecisionAnalysis` subsection and the framework architecture doc's `DecisionAnalysis` references must land in the same commit as the code change. Verify with a grep over both files for `DecisionAnalysis`, `analysis.category`, `analysis.stakes`, `analysis.confidence`, `analysis.observable`, and the trailing phrase "and analysis" after the edit.

## Decisions

| # | Decision | Default | Rationale |
|---|----------|---------|-----------|
| D1 | Remove the guild-wide `tags?: string[]` field from Spider's `ChoiceQuestionSpec` / `BooleanQuestionSpec` / `TextQuestionSpec`? | keep | Spider's `feedback.js` actively consumes `tags` for filter chips in the patron review UI — guild-wide type with non-astrolabe consumers. Only drop astrolabe's use of the field. |
| D2 | How to satisfy the verification "a surfaced question has no `tags` property set by astrolabe"? | add-fresh-test | A dedicated named test makes the post-removal invariant visible and guards against accidental reintroduction. |
| D3 | Strip only tag assertions, or delete the tag-asserting test cases wholesale? | delete-all | Every tag test is single-purpose — it exists only to exercise the tag path. Stripping assertions would leave empty shells. Remove the `// ── Analysis tags tests ──` banner with them. |
| D4 | Rewrite the `PlanDoc.decisions` JSDoc "Architectural/design decisions with options and analysis."? | rewrite | After removal the trailing "and analysis" reads as a stale field reference. Shorten to "Architectural/design decisions with options." in both `packages/plugins/astrolabe/src/types.ts` and `docs/architecture/apparatus/astrolabe.md`. |

## Acceptance Signal

- `pnpm -w typecheck` passes.
- `pnpm -w test` passes, including a fresh decision-review engine test that asserts `'tags' in q === false` on a surfaced `ChoiceQuestionSpec`.
- A repo-wide grep for `DecisionAnalysis`, `buildAnalysisTags`, `CONFIDENCE_TAGS`, `STAKES_TAGS`, `CATEGORY_TAGS`, `OBSERVABLE_TAGS`, and `decisionAnalysisSchema` returns zero hits.
- A repo-wide grep for the phrase `and analysis` within JSDoc on `PlanDoc.decisions` returns zero hits across `packages/plugins/astrolabe/src/types.ts` and `docs/architecture/apparatus/astrolabe.md`.
- The astrolabe plugin README no longer contains the `Decision` + `DecisionAnalysis` subsection, the interface snippet, or the analysis→tags mapping table.
- The decision-review engine still surfaces `ChoiceQuestionSpec` entries for reviewable decisions; the `tags` property is simply absent from those entries.
- The `ChoiceQuestionSpec.tags?` field on Spider's question-spec types is unchanged (unrelated guild consumers still depend on it).

## Existing Patterns

- **Inline Zod schemas per tool.** Other write-tool validators in `packages/plugins/astrolabe/src/astrolabe.ts` (e.g., `scopeWriteTool` and the non-analysis portions of `decisionsWriteTool`) define their Zod shape inline via `z.array(z.object({...}))`. Removing the extracted `decisionAnalysisSchema` returns the file to this prevailing one-schema-per-tool convention.
- **Test-fixture minimalism.** `packages/plugins/astrolabe/src/engines.test.ts` already contains many `Decision[]` fixtures that omit `analysis` and exercise the non-tag code path (e.g., the "blocks on first run with decisions and scope items" test). Use those as the template for the fresh test added per D2.
- **Doc-tracks-code invariant.** The plugin README and the framework architecture doc at `docs/architecture/apparatus/astrolabe.md` are updated alongside the code in the same commit, never deferred.

## What NOT To Do

- Do NOT modify the sage instruction files (`sage-analyst.md`, `sage-reading-analyst.md`) — they are the trigger for this cleanup and are already updated.
- Do NOT touch any other field on `Decision` (`selected`, `patronOverride`, `recommendation`, `rationale`, `scope`, `question`, `options`).
- Do NOT remove or alter the `tags?: string[]` field on Spider's `ChoiceQuestionSpec`, `BooleanQuestionSpec`, or `TextQuestionSpec` — Spider's feedback UI consumes it.
- Do NOT touch the razor, Reach Test, or Patch Test prose in any sage instruction file.
- Do NOT introduce a deprecation alias, compatibility shim, or `@deprecated` JSDoc — this is a clean deletion.
- Do NOT retire or edit the "Status: Draft" banner on the architecture doc (noted as potentially stale in observations; that is a follow-up, not current scope).
- Do NOT refactor neighboring decision-review code (summary builder, reconcile branch, razor logic) — they do not touch `analysis` and stay as-is.

    "id": "w-mo2elhqm-1767759751cd",
    "type": "mandate",
    "status": "completed",
    "title": "Writ Link Meaning Substrate",
    "body": "# Writ Link Meaning Substrate

## Intent

Add a first-class `semanticMeaning` layer to writ links so that load-bearing relationships (parent/child, refines, supersedes) can be identified by stable, plugin-owned meaning ids while keeping the existing open-string `type` field for casual free-form labeling. Introduce a deterministic normalization pipeline for `type` so that the twelve observed spellings of the same relationship collapse to one canonical string, and a kit-contributed meaning registry that lets plugins reserve and document the load-bearing vocabulary.

## Rationale

Today `WritLinkDoc.type` is an open string and every caller invents their own spelling — `fixes`, `Fixes`, `depends-on`, `dependsOn`, `depends_on`, `depends on` all produce distinct links. That's fine for incidental labels but destroys any mechanism that wants to react to a *specific* relationship (rendering parent/child, gating publish on `refines`, hydrating a cross-reference panel). The semantic-meaning layer gives plugins a way to reserve canonical ids for the relationships they care about; the normalization pipeline cleans up the casual-label noise so identical-intent links actually identify as equal.

## Scope & Blast Radius

Primary package: `packages/plugins/clerk/`. Every surface of the link substrate is touched — the `WritLinkDoc` shape, the `link()`/`unlink()` API, the composite-id construction, the books registry, `start()`-time migration, the kit registry wiring, new tools, and the test suite.

Secondary surfaces flow automatically:

- **`packages/framework/cli/`** is NOT edited. New tools named in the clerk package auto-register under `nsg writ ...` via the existing tool→CLI mapping. The auto-grouping rule (split on first hyphen) must be respected when naming tools.
